title: What Won: Democracy or Moneyocracy?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. `பணநாயகம்`, Kalaignar's contrast with `ஜனநாயகம்`, is rendered as “moneyocracy” to retain the title's wordplay about rule by money. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy; the frozen source chapter remains unchanged and authoritative. The source images restore the OCR-omitted opening `உடன்பிறப்பே,`; clearly mechanical OCR character errors and word breaks are repaired. Newspaper editorials and allegations remain explicitly attributed. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: The local-body by-elections have been held in Tamil Nadu. All the arrangements were completed beforehand, and, just as expected, the AIADMK has won. Ah! Do you know the magnificent extent to which democracy raised its flag, gleamed and flew in that election? Do you know the reports that appeared in the newspapers? I myself have commented on it two or three times.

There is an old saying: “When you see the wicked, keep your distance.” Likewise, when there is a by-election under AIADMK rule, the principal opposition parties in Tamil Nadu correctly anticipate that authoritarian performances and lawless acts will be staged everywhere. As though saying, “What have we to do with this?”, they boycotted the election itself.

The DMK, DMDK, MDMK, PMK, Viduthalai Chiruthaigal Katchi, Congress, Indian Union Muslim League, Manithaneya Makkal Katchi and Puthiya Tamilagam all stayed away. They already knew the kinds of manipulation in which the AIADMK would engage during a by-election.

To begin with, did the Election Commission announce the date properly? On 6 August, the Tamil Nadu Election Commission announced that the local-body by-elections would take place on 18 September. Yet, for some unexplained reason, the same Commission withdrew its announcement on the very day it was issued!

Twenty-two days later, on 28 August, the Commission again announced the by-elections. When announcing the election after a delay of twenty-two days, should it not have changed the date previously announced? But it did not. It again declared that polling would take place on 18 September! Has such absurdity ever occurred at the very outset of any election anywhere? Why did they retain that date? We are told it was an “auspicious” day for the Chief Minister!

Ultimately, only the BJP and Communist parties came forward to contest against the ruling party and filed nominations. But did the ruling party permit even them to contest democratically? Here are one or two examples.

Mayoral elections were announced in three corporations: Tirunelveli, Thoothukudi and Coimbatore. In Tirunelveli, they ensured that no election took place at all. How? Many nominations filed against the ruling party were rejected on one lame pretext or another. Those whose nominations could not be rejected were “managed”: what had to be done was done, what had to be said was said, and every opposing nomination was made to be withdrawn. On the final day, even the principal BJP candidate withdrew her nomination, and then the candidate herself was admitted into the AIADMK! Was this full-length comedy staged only in Tirunelveli?

By-elections were held for Wards 1 and 6 of Chengalpattu Municipality. Municipal Commissioner and Election Officer Muthu Venkatesh rejected the nominations of independent candidates Thanigavelu and Basiyulla, who contested against the AIADMK, on the ground that they had not filed income-tax returns. He then declared the two AIADMK candidates elected and issued certificates to them.

The independent candidates challenged this in the Madras High Court. The Judge ordered an inquiry into the election officials. The inquiry established that the Municipal Commissioner had unlawfully rejected the nominations, and a report was submitted to the Judge. The Judge ruled that the AIADMK candidates’ victories were invalid and ordered fresh elections. With no other course available, the Election Commission suspended Municipal Commissioner Muthu Venkatesh on 21 September 2014. Whether this suspension is mere eyewash, a drama staged to deceive the court, will emerge later!
Do you know what happened in Coimbatore?
The BJP candidate there was Nandakumar. Unlike what happened in Tirunelveli, he refused to accept whatever was offered and withdraw his nomination. What followed? He was attacked.

On the morning of 17 September 2014, the day before polling, Nandakumar and members of his party heard that AIADMK members were distributing money to voters in the Sowripalayam area of Ward 65. They went there, caught several people red-handed, including Bodipalayam Panchayat Union Chairman Palanisamy, as they allegedly tried to distribute money, and took them to the police station.

On hearing this, more than one hundred AIADMK members gathered and attacked the BJP candidate. BJP members surrounded and protected him and took him to a car. The AIADMK group then surrounded the car, broke its windows and created a disturbance. Did this happen or not?

In Pudukkottai, BJP and Communist candidates were not even allowed to file nominations for Municipal Chairperson. Their road blockade and protests produced no result. Having prevented opponents from filing nominations, officials declared the AIADMK victorious.

What was the position in Chennai? By 11 a.m., ruling-party members had captured every polling booth in Chennai Corporation Division 35. A CPI(M) candidate was contesting against the ruling party. When party members complained to election officials, the officials reportedly refused to intervene.

In the Cuddalore municipal election, CPI(M) Chairperson candidate Madhavan petitioned the State Election Commissioner and District Collector. He alleged that AIADMK members violated election rules, captured booths and cast bogus votes, expelled opposition agents and issued death threats. He sought a fresh election for the Cuddalore Municipal Chairperson’s post.
A by-election was held in Ramanathapuram because Municipal Chairperson Sekar had died. Yet the vote of that dead Chairperson was reportedly cast in the by-election. Could there be a worse fraud?

Of the 3,075 people who filed nominations in these by-elections, 1,589 nominations were rejected or made to be withdrawn. Is any further evidence needed to show the enormous scale of the irregularities?

They bound the hands and feet of the opposition and locked their mouths. Then they claimed to conduct an election, engaged in every kind of fraud and irregularity, and beat their chests about having won. Is that victory? Was such a degrading victory necessary?
That is why even the neutral weekly *Kalki*, deeply distressed, wrote an editorial. Its main passages follow:

“The local-body by-election in Tamil Nadu has created profound revulsion among opposition parties and in the public mind. The principal opposition parties, the DMK, MDMK and DMDK, boycotted it because the very manner in which the election was announced was improper. The BJP and Communist parties entered the field to provide space for alternative public opinion. But reports arriving from many places are far from encouraging.

“Union Minister Pon. Radhakrishnan said: ‘In many local bodies, the ruling party used the police to prevent BJP candidates from even contesting. Many nominations filed despite this were rejected on false grounds. Wherever BJP nominations were accepted, every method was employed to make the candidates withdraw.’ Going a step further, he said that money was distributed to voters in many areas. These are clear examples of by-election irregularities. CPI(M) and CPI members protested, alleging that opposition parties were prevented from contesting.

“In many places, the ruling party boasts of an unopposed election. What honour can attach to a victory obtained after creating circumstances in which other parties cannot properly face the election? The reason for such misconduct is fear of defeat. After winning the parliamentary election, the ruling party has become possessed by a desire for one hundred per cent victory.

“If people’s livelihood needs had been met and arrangements made for them to live independently, there would be no need to fear defeat. But once misconduct is proved, even the ruling party’s earlier victories become suspect. A shadow of doubt inevitably falls even upon the thirty-seven parliamentary seats it won.”
That is *Kalki*’s editorial.
Even *Theekkathir*, the newspaper of the CPI(M), which maintained friendly relations with the AIADMK until last year, wrote in today’s editorial:

“The by-election result constitutes a grave defeat for democracy. AIADMK members trampled every democratic practice and election rule underfoot in the local-body election. From the moment the election date was announced, the ruling party’s exploits began with the assistance of the State Election Commission.

“It is no exaggeration to say that the ruling party followed every method described as conciliation, division, inducement and punishment. In each place, it degraded democracy in a different way. The truth is that the ruling party has made democracy hang its head in shame.”
The ruling party’s excesses during this election appear to prove every word in these editorials true.

How did Ministers camp in the places where polling occurred? How much money was distributed? In how many ways was repression unleashed? The Chief Minister herself said she could not see any opponents, yet travelled by air to campaign. What costly arrangements were made for her visit? Were they all necessary?

Ministers feared that if the party lost, their posts would be taken away. They were therefore driven to spend substantial sums from the money they had accumulated. They had the confidence that if their posts continued, they could recover what they spent!
After paying such an enormous price, can what they obtained be called victory? Would not even the elected members of that party laugh?

We are told: “Chief Minister Amma thanks the great voters who delivered a historic victory! A flood of happiness! Firecrackers burst and sweets distributed!” Are firecrackers and sweets not an absurdity for a victory obtained through an alliance between the ruling AIADMK and the State Election Commissioner, assisted by the police?

In a wrestling match, they disabled every opponent and brought the referee under their control. Does the ruling party’s victory now possess any meaning or legitimacy? If conditions continue unhindered in this fashion, democracy will soon be found only in a museum!
What won the local-body by-election: democracy or moneyocracy?
With affection,
M.K.
24 September 2014
